era_context: Middle Bronze Age II; Egyptian 12th Dynasty in its mature or late phase; Old Babylonian period; Indus Valley Civilization; early Shang or Xia Dynasty in China (dating highly uncertain). Urban centers, long-distance trade, and centralized state administration characterize this era across multiple civilizations.

events.title: Continued prosperity and building activity in Egypt under 12th Dynasty
events.description: The 12th Dynasty of Egypt, particularly under Amenemhat III, was characterized by extensive architectural projects, administrative efficiency, and economic prosperity. In 1952 BCE, ongoing construction projects, temple maintenance, and administrative records would have been routine. The period saw sophisticated art, literature (including the Tale of Sinuhe), and bureaucratic organization. Nubian trade and military campaigns continued to secure Egypt's southern borders.
key_figures: High officials and priests of major temples

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from temple sites, tombs, and settlement excavations
Papyri and ostraca with administrative records

events.certainty_note: Year-by-year precision is not achievable for this period. Assignment to 1952 BCE is conventional; actual dating may vary by ±20–30 years depending on chronological system.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are reliably documented for the year 1952 BCE. The assignment of events to this particular year is largely arbitrary, as ancient sources do not provide year-by-year narratives for most of the Bronze Age. Modern chronologies are reconstructed from king lists, astronomical calculations, and radiocarbon dating, all of which carry significant margins of error. The 'prosperity' attributed to the 12th Dynasty is a long-term characterization, not a year-specific claim.
historiographic_note: For 1952 BCE, the historical record is almost entirely dependent on archaeological material culture and administrative documents that are not precisely dated to individual years. Written narratives from this period are rare and typically composed centuries after the events they describe. The bias toward Egyptian and Mesopotamian sources means that the vast majority of human activity—in Africa, Asia, Europe, and the Americas—is invisible in the historical record. Any year-by-year narrative for this period is largely a scholarly construction rather than a reflection of preserved historical documentation.
